text: The surgeon begins the operation by opening the porta hepatis, exposing the hepatic artery for identification.
The surgeon removes lymph nodes from the hepatic artery region, collecting them as specimens.
The surgeon cuts the connections from the common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, and right hepatic artery to prevent extrapatic perfusion from the hepatic artery infusion pump.
The surgeon encircles the gastroduodenal artery, preparing to ligate a branch that extends from the right side of the artery.
The surgeon ties a suture around the gastroduodenal artery to secure it, ensuring it does not obstruct the placement of the pump catheter during the procedure.
The surgeon encircles the common hepatic artery completely, ensuring it is isolated to prevent extrahepatic perfusion from the pump.
The surgeon continues dissection up to the bifurcation of the right and left hepatic arteries, focusing additional dissection on the right hepatic artery to address potential extrahepatic perfusion.
The surgeon performs a mini Cochrane maneuver to ensure there is no extra hepatic perfusion, focusing on the lateral side of the porta hepatis.
The surgeon clips the cystic duct and artery before removing the gallbladder from its fossa to prevent chemical cholecystitis during chemotherapy pump use.
A papaverine-soaked sponge is placed on top of the gastroduodenal artery and hepatic artery, visually covering these structures.
The surgeon creates a pump pocket in the left upper quadrant and inserts the pump catheter through the abdominal wall into the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon ligates the gastrodontal artery, securing it to prevent blood flow.
Bulldog clamps are placed on the common and proper hepatic artery, visibly occluding blood flow to these vessels.
Under direct vision, the surgeon inserts an eleven blade into the abdomen and applies tension on the gastrointestinal artery to perform a precise arteriotomy.
The surgeon extracts the blade and inserts a vein retractor into the surgical field, likely to manipulate or expose vascular structures within the abdomen.

The surgeon inserts the pump catheter into the arteriotomy and advances it to the junction of the common hepatic artery and the gastroduodenal artery, ensuring precise placement within the vascular structures.
Silk ties are used to secure the catheter in place against the distal bevel of the pump catheter, ensuring stability within the surgical field.
After securing the first silk tie, the surgeon removes the bulldog clamps from the common and proper hepatic artery. Heparin saline is then flushed through the pump catheter to ensure its patency.
The surgeon places two additional silk ties around the pump catheter and the gastroduodenal artery, ensuring the catheter remains patent by flushing it with heparin saline after each tie.

Blue dye is injected, causing the liver to illuminate blue. A small area of extrahepatic perfusion is observed in the duodenum, which is corrected by ligating branches running anterior to the common bile duct.
The surgeon performs additional dissection on the right side of the porta hepatis to address the extrahepatic perfusion issue, carefully manipulating the tissue around this area.
The surgeon performs a repeat blue dye test, showing no blue dye filling in the duodenum, indicating successful ligation of the previously identified extra hepatic perfusion.
The ultrasound image of the liver shows the segment three co-rectal liver metastasis, which is a known finding.
The surgeon marks a resection line on the liver capsule, ensuring a gross margin greater than one centimeter from the targeted area.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer for parenchymal dissection of the liver, with the option to utilize bipolar and robotic harmonic devices for this procedure.
The round ligament is ligated at the completion of the hepatectomy. The video shows the pump and the port sites used during the procedure.
